text: The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to perform a lower ligation on the iliac colic pedicle, avoiding the more central superior mesenteric vessels. Large matted nodes are visible beneath the vessel sealer.

The surgeon observes a fibrotic and scarred retroperitoneal plane, with the mid-ascending colon adhered to the side wall, suggesting acute inflammation rather than malignancy. Pus is visible, indicating a perforated retrocecal appendix, with the tip of the appendix exposed. The perforation appears unrelated to cancer, suggesting appendicitis.

The surgeon attempts to detach the mid-ascending colon from the sidewall, performing lateral mobilization without ureteral catheters. The hepatic flexure is mobilized, and the surgeon joins previous lateral mobilization efforts to address the perforated adhesed segment. The right colon mesentery is dissected, avoiding high ligation, focusing on tumor removal rather than nodal involvement.
The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the colon, taking additional mesentery to ensure clean margins. The perforated appendix is visible hanging down from the surgical field.

The surgeon staples the super pubic region and the small bowel, aligning an isoparastaltic anastomosis. A colotomy and small bowel enterotomy are visible, followed by the closure of these openings with sutures.
The surgeon finishes closing the common channel without leaving a drain, and upsizes the twelve millimeter trocar site for phanaceal extraction.
